stimulus: In the years following the Korean War, some young Korean American women in Los Angeles adopted a striking new look that newspapers of the time called the kkang style. They wore boxy, oversized blazers with rolled cuffs, dramatic eyeliner, and short, sharply cut bangs, often paired with motorcycle boots. Some commentators criticized the kkang style at the time, saying it was reckless and that the young women ought to dress more conventionally. But historians of Asian American fashion see things differently. They view the kkang style as a form of resistance to the era's rigid expectations about gender and respectability. They argue that it expressed the young women's desire for personal expression and autonomy.
stem: According to the text, how do historians of Asian American fashion view the kkang style?

answerOptions.content: They think the kkang style allowed young Korean American women to display their support for U.S. military efforts in the Korean War.
They think the kkang style closely resembled fashion trends adopted by other groups of young women at the time.
They think the kkang style was a way for some young Korean American women to express themselves and resist conservative expectations.
They think the kkang style was so influential that it continues to shape fashion in Los Angeles to the present day.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The text contrasts contemporary critics' view of the kkang style with that of historians of Asian American fashion. Historians, the text says, see the style as a form of resistance to rigid expectations about gender and respectability and as an expression of personal autonomy. According to the text, then, historians view the kkang style as a way for some young Korean American women to express themselves and resist conservative expectations.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not connect the kkang style to support for the war. Choice B is incorrect because the text discusses no other fashion trends. Choice D is incorrect because the text presents the style as belonging to a specific postwar moment, not as a continuing influence on fashion.
